title: Intermediate Evidence Review
description: Evaluate claims, spot limits in evidence, and choose careful conclusions.

questions.prompt: A class tried a new review method. Average scores rose from 70 to 78. What is the most careful conclusion?
choices: Scores improved after the method, but more evidence is needed to prove the method caused it
The method caused every student to improve
The method had no possible relationship to the scores
The scores prove the next class will average 90
questions.hint: Before-and-after evidence is useful but limited.
questions.explanation: The scores improved, but without more controls or context, causation should be stated carefully.

questions.prompt: A survey includes only three people from a group of two hundred. What is the main limitation?
choices: The sample may be too small to represent the full group
The survey has too many participants
The results must be perfectly accurate
The group size no longer matters
questions.hint: Ask whether the evidence represents the larger group.
questions.explanation: A very small sample can miss important variation in the full group.

questions.prompt: When two sources disagree, the stronger source is always the one that sounds more confident.
questions.explanation: Source strength depends on evidence, method, relevance, and reliability, not just confident wording.

questions.prompt: A hidden factor that may affect both the supposed cause and the result is a _____.
questions.hint: It can confuse the relationship being studied.
questions.explanation: A confounder is an outside factor that can distort a cause-and-effect interpretation.

questions.prompt: A learner's practice scores are 7, 8, 9, 3, and 10. What should an analyst notice first?
choices: There is a generally rising pattern with one unusually low score
The scores are all identical
The final score is the lowest
There is no information to compare
questions.hint: Look for both the trend and the unusual value.
questions.explanation: The scores mostly rise, while the 3 stands out as a possible outlier or special case.

questions.prompt: A claim says, 'This strategy works for everyone,' but the evidence comes from one learner. What is the issue?
choices: The claim is broader than the evidence supports
The evidence is too broad for the claim
One learner proves all possible cases
The claim is automatically false because it is short
questions.hint: Compare the size of the claim with the size of the evidence.
questions.explanation: Evidence from one learner can be useful, but it cannot justify a universal claim by itself.

questions.prompt: Which evidence would best test whether spaced practice helped a class?
choices: Scores from comparable learners using spaced and non-spaced practice
The color of the classroom walls
A single comment from someone who did not practice
The number of pages in the textbook only
questions.hint: A comparison helps isolate the practice method.
questions.explanation: Comparable groups using different practice schedules would give stronger evidence about the effect of spacing.

questions.prompt: An outlier should be deleted automatically before anyone asks why it happened.
questions.hint: Unusual data can be a mistake, but it can also be meaningful.
questions.explanation: Outliers should be investigated before deciding whether they are errors or important signals.

questions.prompt: A chart shows completion rate but not score quality. What question remains unanswered?
choices: Whether learners completed the quiz accurately
Whether anyone opened the quiz
Whether the quiz has a title
Whether the chart uses numbers
questions.hint: Completion and quality are related but not identical.
questions.explanation: A completion rate shows who finished, but it does not show whether their answers were correct.

questions.prompt: A reasonable explanation that could also fit the evidence is an alternative _____.
questions.hint: It is another way to explain the same evidence.
questions.explanation: Considering alternative explanations helps avoid jumping to a single unsupported cause.

questions.prompt: Which wording is most appropriate when evidence is suggestive but not conclusive?
choices: The evidence suggests a possible relationship
The evidence proves the relationship in every case
No relationship can exist
The question cannot be discussed
questions.hint: Careful wording should match the strength of the evidence.
questions.explanation: Suggestive evidence supports cautious language, not absolute proof.

questions.prompt: A learner improves after getting tutoring and also begins sleeping more. What is the best analysis move?
choices: Consider that tutoring, sleep, or both may have contributed
Assume tutoring is the only possible cause
Assume sleep is impossible to consider
Ignore both changes
questions.hint: More than one change happened at the same time.
questions.explanation: When multiple factors change, analysis should consider more than one possible contributor.
